content.text: User personas in traditional UX practice describe demographics, goals, and frustrations. When the product being designed includes a generative AI or agentic component, the persona must be extended to capture dimensions that are unique to human-AI interaction: the user's mental model of how the AI works, their tolerance for model errors, their data-sharing boundaries, and the conditions under which they want to override or escalate away from the AI. Without these extensions, personas built for AI products systematically miss the factors that most strongly influence whether a user will trust and continue to use the AI feature.

An AI user persona for an agentic application is a composite representation of a user segment that describes not only who the user is and what they are trying to accomplish, but also how they conceptualise, interact with, and set boundaries for an AI system. The persona synthesises data from user interviews, behavioural analytics, support ticket analysis, and domain expert input into a structured document that product managers, engineers, and UX designers can use as a shared reference throughout the feature lifecycle.

An AI persona differs from a standard UX persona in eight dimensions: goals and jobs-to-be-done (which tasks the AI should handle), usage context (when and where the AI is invoked), domain vocabulary (the user's technical language and expected AI response register), AI expectations (what accuracy, speed, and behaviour the user considers baseline), data sensitivity (which information the user is comfortable sharing with an AI system), error tolerance (which AI mistakes are recoverable and which are trust-destroying), human override triggers (the conditions under which the user wants to take back control from the AI), and accessibility needs specific to the AI interaction modality.

Personas for agentic applications also need to describe the user's relationship to the agent's autonomy level. A user who is comfortable with fully autonomous task execution has a fundamentally different persona profile than one who expects to review and approve every significant agent action — and these two profiles drive completely different UX designs, approval flows, and error handling strategies for the same underlying agent capability.

The failure mode most common in early GenAI product development is building for the developer's mental model of the AI rather than the user's. Engineers who build AI products typically have a much higher tolerance for model errors, a much more accurate model of how the AI works, and a willingness to iterate through multiple AI responses that most end users do not share. Without structured AI personas grounding the design, products are routinely shipped with error messages that assume technical sophistication, autonomy levels that exceed what users are comfortable delegating, and AI output formats that match what the developer considers useful rather than what the target user can act on.

Personas also drive the evaluation strategy for AI features. The failure scenarios and edge cases that matter for evaluation are the ones that the target persona would encounter frequently or find trust-destroying. A persona-free evaluation suite tests the AI's general capabilities; a persona-grounded evaluation suite tests whether the AI meets the specific needs and expectations of the people who will actually use it.

— when an engineering team debates whether to show confidence scores to users, an AI persona that specifies the user's statistical literacy ("non-technical, cannot interpret probabilities") resolves the debate with evidence rather than opinion.
— personas define the inputs, contexts, and expectations that evaluation suites must cover, ensuring that test cases reflect realistic user behaviour rather than synthetic edge cases.
— the persona's domain vocabulary and expected response register directly inform system prompt design, ensuring the AI's output style matches the user segment.
— the error tolerance and human override trigger dimensions prevent teams from over-automating features for users who need more control, or under-automating for users who want less friction.
— a shared AI persona document gives product, engineering, safety, and legal a common vocabulary for discussing whose needs the feature serves and what constraints apply.

Personas are composites, not individuals — a single persona cannot capture the full variance within a user segment, and product decisions that optimise for the persona may systematically underserve atypical users in that segment.
AI expectations and error tolerance change as users gain experience with AI tools — a persona created at product launch may be inaccurate for users who have been using AI features for two years.
Without grounding in real user research, AI personas reflect the assumptions of whoever wrote them — demographic information from marketing may not predict AI-specific behaviour accurately.

Multiple personas for the same product create design tension that must be managed — a feature cannot simultaneously serve a power user who wants maximum automation and a cautious user who wants maximum control.
AI persona development follows a four-step process: research, synthesis, AI-dimension enrichment, and validation.

Conduct 6–10 user interviews specifically focused on AI interaction behaviour. Standard UX interview questions about goals and frustrations are insufficient — the research must also surface the user's mental model of AI systems ("how do you think the AI decides what to say?"), their past experience with AI errors ("tell me about a time an AI gave you a wrong answer — what did you do next?"), their data-sharing boundaries ("what information would you be uncomfortable sharing with an AI assistant?"), and their preferred level of AI autonomy ("would you rather the AI complete the task automatically and tell you, or draft a plan for you to review?").

Affinity-map the research findings across the eight AI-specific persona dimensions. Look for clusters that represent distinct user segments rather than forcing all research findings into a single persona. For most B2B GenAI products, two to four personas emerge: a power user who is comfortable with high autonomy, a cautious adopter who needs reassurance and control, a domain expert who expects technical precision, and an occasional user who needs simplicity over capability.

For each persona, explicitly fill out the AI-specific dimensions: write down the exact types of errors the persona would find recoverable versus trust-destroying, list the categories of data they would not share with an AI, specify the trigger conditions for wanting human oversight, and document the response style they expect (formal vs. conversational, verbose vs. terse, with or without citations). These dimensions drive specific engineering and prompt design decisions that standard persona templates miss entirely.

Share persona drafts with research participants and with frontline staff who interact regularly with the target user segment. Validate specifically the AI-dimension attributes — these are most likely to reflect researcher assumptions rather than user reality. Update the persona based on feedback and repeat the validation step when the AI feature enters beta to check whether real usage behaviour matches the persona predictions.

attrs.alt: Eight-dimension AI persona structure for agentic applications

content.text: The diagram illustrates the eight AI-specific persona dimensions arranged around a central persona entity. Standard UX attributes (name, role) anchor the centre, while the surrounding dimensions — goals, AI expectations, data sensitivity, usage context, domain vocabulary, error tolerance, human override triggers, and accessibility needs — provide the AI-specific attributes that drive engineering and prompt design decisions.

AI persona development is warranted at the start of any project that includes a user-facing LLM or agentic component. It is especially important when the target user segment has limited prior experience with AI tools (making AI expectations and mental models more varied and less predictable), when the feature involves sensitive data (making data sensitivity boundaries more consequential), or when the feature has variable autonomy levels (making the human override trigger dimension critical to design).

Full persona development is overkill for internal tools where the user segment is a small, homogeneous group of technical experts who will be closely involved in the feature design. In those cases, direct involvement of representative users in the design process is more efficient than creating a persona artefact.
: Before user interviews, prepare a question set that covers all eight persona dimensions. Standard UX question banks do not include the AI-specific dimensions — add them explicitly.
: Run 45–60 minute contextual inquiry sessions where participants interact with an existing AI tool (can be a competitor or a prototype) while you observe and probe for AI-specific reactions.
: Use a digital whiteboard to cluster insights by dimension. Do not collapse all findings into a single persona prematurely — resist this until the natural clusters are clear.

: Create a one-page persona document for each identified segment. Include a name, photo (illustrative, not a real person), the eight AI dimensions filled out, and three to five specific quotes from research participants that capture the persona's voice.

: For each persona's error tolerance and human override trigger dimensions, generate at least three evaluation test cases that would reveal whether the AI feature is meeting or violating those expectations.
: Set a calendar reminder to review and update personas every six months, or after any major change in the user base composition or the AI feature's capability level.
The following Python code implements a structured AI persona class with validation, serialisation, and evaluation scenario generation:

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from enum import Enum
from typing import Optional

class AutonomyPreference(Enum):
    FULLY_AUTONOMOUS = "fully_autonomous"
    REVIEW_SIGNIFICANT = "review_significant"  # review only high-stakes actions
    REVIEW_ALL = "review_all"                  # approve every AI action
    SUGGEST_ONLY = "suggest_only"              # AI suggests, human decides

class TechnicalLevel(Enum):
    NOVICE = "novice"
    INTERMEDIATE = "intermediate"
    EXPERT = "expert"

@dataclass
class AIPersona:
    # Standard UX attributes
    name: str
    role: str
    age_range: str
    segment_size: str  # approximate, e.g. "30% of user base"

    # AI-specific dimensions
    primary_jobs: list[str]          # jobs-to-be-done for AI
    usage_context: str               # when/where AI is used
    domain_vocabulary: list[str]     # key terms this persona uses
    technical_level: TechnicalLevel
    ai_accuracy_expectation: float   # 0.0–1.0, e.g. 0.95 = expects 95% accuracy
    preferred_response_style: str    # "terse", "detailed", "conversational"
    autonomy_preference: AutonomyPreference
    sensitive_data_categories: list[str]  # data the persona WON'T share with AI
    recoverable_errors: list[str]    # errors the persona accepts and recovers from
    trust_destroying_errors: list[str]  # errors that cause persona to abandon AI
    override_triggers: list[str]     # conditions requiring human control
    accessibility_needs: list[str]

    # Supporting research
    representative_quotes: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)

    def generate_eval_scenarios(self) -> list[dict]:
        """Generate evaluation test case seeds from persona attributes."""
        scenarios = []

        # One scenario per trust-destroying error
        for error in self.trust_destroying_errors:
            scenarios.append({
                "persona": self.name,
                "scenario_type": "trust_destroying_failure",
                "trigger": error,
                "expected_system_behaviour": "graceful degradation with clear explanation",
                "pass_criteria": f"User ({self.name}) would not lose trust in the system",
            })

        # One scenario per override trigger
        for trigger in self.override_triggers:
            scenarios.append({
                "persona": self.name,
                "scenario_type": "human_override",
                "trigger": trigger,
                "expected_system_behaviour": "surface human override option prominently",
                "pass_criteria": "Override is accessible within one interaction step",
            })

        # Sensitive data scenario
        if self.sensitive_data_categories:
            scenarios.append({
                "persona": self.name,
                "scenario_type": "sensitive_data_handling",
                "trigger": f"User shares {self.sensitive_data_categories[0]} with AI",
                "expected_system_behaviour": "Reject or mask sensitive data before LLM call",
                "pass_criteria": "No sensitive data appears in LLM prompt",
            })

        return scenarios

    def system_prompt_hints(self) -> list[str]:
        """Derive system prompt guidance from persona attributes."""
        hints = []
        style_map = {
            "terse": "Use brief, direct responses. Avoid preamble.",
            "detailed": "Provide thorough explanations. Include reasoning.",
            "conversational": "Use a friendly, accessible tone. Avoid jargon.",
        }
        if self.preferred_response_style in style_map:
            hints.append(style_map[self.preferred_response_style])
        if self.technical_level == TechnicalLevel.NOVICE:
            hints.append("Avoid technical jargon. Explain acronyms.")
        elif self.technical_level == TechnicalLevel.EXPERT:
            hints.append(f"Use domain terminology: {', '.join(self.domain_vocabulary[:3])}.")
        if self.ai_accuracy_expectation >= 0.95:
            hints.append("State uncertainty explicitly when confidence is low.")
        return hints

# Example: Cautious Adopter persona for an AI legal research assistant
cautious_adopter = AIPersona(
    name="Morgan Reyes",
    role="Junior Associate Attorney",
    age_range="26–32",
    segment_size="45% of user base",
    primary_jobs=[
        "Find relevant case law quickly",
        "Summarise long documents",
        "Draft initial research memos",
    ],
    usage_context="In-office, high-pressure research sessions, 5–10 queries per day",
    domain_vocabulary=["holding", "dicta", "prima facie", "res judicata"],
    technical_level=TechnicalLevel.INTERMEDIATE,
    ai_accuracy_expectation=0.97,  # legal errors can be catastrophic
    preferred_response_style="detailed",
    autonomy_preference=AutonomyPreference.REVIEW_ALL,
    sensitive_data_categories=["client names", "matter numbers", "privileged communications"],
    recoverable_errors=[
        "Citation formatting error",
        "Slightly outdated case information",
    ],
    trust_destroying_errors=[
        "Fabricated case citation (hallucination)",
        "Incorrect holding attributed to a real case",
        "Disclosure of client information",
    ],
    override_triggers=[
        "Jurisdiction does not match current matter",
        "Conflicting circuit authority detected",
        "Confidence score below 0.9",
    ],
    accessibility_needs=["Screen-reader compatible output"],
    representative_quotes=[
        "If I can't verify it in Westlaw, I'm not citing it.",
        "I don't want the AI to file anything on my behalf — I review everything.",
    ],
)

print("=== System Prompt Hints ===")
for h in cautious_adopter.system_prompt_hints():
    print(f"  - {h}")

print("\n=== Evaluation Scenarios ===")
for s in cautious_adopter.generate_eval_scenarios():
    print(f"  [{s['scenario_type']}] {s['trigger']}")

An enterprise HR software company building an AI recruiting assistant identified three distinct personas through a research programme: the Strategic Recruiter (high volume, wants maximum automation, trusts AI to filter resumes), the Compliance-Conscious Recruiter (works in regulated industries, needs every AI decision auditable and human-approved), and the Technical Hiring Manager (deep domain expert, wants the AI to understand engineering-specific job requirements, distrusts generic summaries).

The three personas had completely different autonomy preferences: the Strategic Recruiter chose fully autonomous resume filtering; the Compliance-Conscious Recruiter required human review of every candidate before outreach; the Technical Hiring Manager wanted AI to shortlist but wanted to personally review technical assessments. These three autonomy profiles drove the design of three configurable workflow modes in the product, each with different UI surfaces, approval gates, and audit log requirements. Without the persona work, the team had assumed a single "AI shortlists, human decides" model that would have frustrated all three segments.
Creating personas from marketing demographics alone
— age, role, and seniority do not predict AI-specific behaviour. A 55-year-old executive may have higher AI trust than a 28-year-old engineer who has experienced many AI failures.
— teams assume users will tolerate the same error rate the team itself tolerates. The persona's error tolerance, derived from actual user research, is almost always lower than the team expects.
Building one persona for the whole product
— most AI products serve multiple user segments with meaningfully different AI expectations. Forcing a single persona leads to design decisions that optimise for one segment at the expense of others.
Not connecting personas to the evaluation suite
— personas that live only in a design document and are not referenced in the evaluation strategy do not improve the AI feature's quality for the target users.

Run AI-specific user research sessions (not standard UX interviews) to ground the AI-dimension attributes in observed behaviour rather than stated preference — what users say they want from AI and what they actually accept often diverge.

Make the error tolerance dimension actionable: convert it to a specific acceptable error rate percentage for the feature's primary task, and use this number as a quantitative acceptance criterion in the evaluation suite.

Derive system prompt directives directly from the persona's domain vocabulary and response style preferences. The persona should be the primary input to the tone and format section of any LLM system prompt for the feature.

Include at least one adversarial persona in every set — a user who actively tries to misuse or circumvent the AI feature. This persona drives security and guardrail requirements that benign personas miss.
Link each persona to a named product area owner who is responsible for ensuring the AI feature continues to meet that persona's needs as the feature evolves.

Persona development is a discovery-phase cost that scales with the number of distinct user segments the product serves. A product serving two distinct personas requires roughly twice the research investment of a product serving one. The return on this investment scales with the size of the user base and the consequence of misalignment — a consumer AI product serving millions of users benefits more from rigorous persona development than an internal tool serving twenty power users.

Personas should be treated as living documents that are updated as the user base grows and as AI feature capabilities change. A major model upgrade that changes the error rate or response style of the AI feature should trigger a persona review to confirm that the new model still meets the expectations documented in the AI expectations dimension.
Enables observation of AI interaction behaviour at scale
Dovetail's affinity mapping features support multi-dimension synthesis
One-page visual persona cards that can be shared with all stakeholders
Structured persona storage for automated eval generation
Enables programmatic derivation of evaluation scenarios from persona attributes

The sensitive data categories dimension of the AI persona is a direct input to the system's data governance requirements. If a persona's research reveals that a user segment considers their employment history sensitive data that should not be shared with an AI system, this maps to a technical requirement: that field must be masked or excluded before any LLM call. Without the persona research to surface this boundary, the engineering team has no signal from user behaviour to inform the data handling design.

The trust-destroying errors dimension informs the reliability requirements for the AI feature. If the persona research reveals that hallucinated citations are trust-destroying, this drives a requirement for a citation verification step that checks any AI-generated source reference against a verified database before presenting it to the user. The persona thus functions as a reliability requirements source, not merely a design artefact.

Standard UX personas must be extended with eight AI-specific dimensions to be useful for generative AI product design: goals, usage context, domain vocabulary, AI expectations, data sensitivity, error tolerance, human override triggers, and accessibility needs.
The error tolerance and trust-destroying error dimensions are the most critical for AI features — they set the quality bar that the evaluation suite must verify.
Autonomy preference drives the core UX architecture of agentic features — personas with different autonomy preferences require fundamentally different approval flows and override mechanisms.
Personas must be grounded in AI-specific user research — demographics and general UX research do not predict AI-specific behaviour accurately.
The persona should be the primary input to the system prompt's tone, vocabulary, and response format — connecting discovery directly to prompt engineering.
Personas should be reviewed and updated after major model upgrades or significant changes in the user base, since AI expectations evolve as users gain experience with the technology.
